body_txt: Though fair ('white') skin has historically been — and in many places still is — sought after, Europe and its offshoots have gone off it lately. This has been due to the increasing popularity of long-distance travel to warm and sunny vacation spots. Throughout the pre-1920s world, fair and un-calloused skin was a sign of being "high-class" and not needing to perform manual labor to survive. Great lengths were taken to maintain pale and dainty skin, including using lead-based makeup from Roman times to roughly the early 17th century, and allegedly arsenic-laced complexion treatments from the 1850s to the early 1900s (usually free of any real arsenic, to cut costs, so some women were saved by unscrupulous manufacturers) and, during certain fashion cycles, ladies carrying parasols whenever going outside . There's a reason that the word "fair" came to be a synonym of "beautiful." Tanned skin was one of the new beauty standards promoted by Coco Chanel in the early 20th century, but it didn't quite take off until the '50s and '60s when people actually had the money and the means for leisurely travel. Tanned skin was a sign that you had both the money and the free time to visit nice places with fresh air and sun, something just not possible for Europe's middle classes in the twenties and thirties . Tanning reached an apex in The '70s , and again in The '90s and the Turn of the Millennium , when every actress and model had to have a dark orange tan in order to be considered attractive. If you couldn't get it in the sun, you could always try sprays or lotions applied to the skin. Tan Lines from swimsuits were not considered unattractive, but a "farmer's tan" — the kind you get by actually working outdoors in a t-shirt and shorts — is still considered a sign of low social class. This also led to the rise of tanning salons, sunbeds, and hours of sun exposure without any form of UV protection, which consequently led to a rise in skin cancer, particularly in young people. Today, while tanning no longer has a chokehold on Western fashion, a light tan is still seen as a sign of a healthy active lifestyle (despite the fact that many dermatologists say there's no such thing as a "safe tan" re: cancer risk), while pale skin is often associated with being a nerd, goth , shut-in , or invalid. People who rarely venture into the sun will often be unwilling to wear shorts or take off their shirt or socks in public for fear of revealing a pasty complexion beneath their standard clothing. Even people who lack the ability to tan (like most gingers) will often be self-conscious of their pale skintone. Meanwhile in some other parts of the world, this trope is nearly nonexistent. In most of Asia, for example, the mainstream view has always been paler is better . Also see Eerie Pale-Skinned Brunette , Undeathly Pallor . Contrast But Not Too Black , the inverse of this trope, but much more severe in the amount of discrimination that it entails. Compare But Not Too Foreign . May be considered a reason why Albinos Are Freaks . Not to be confused with Dark Is Not Evil .
